title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a historian called Sonia Edwards giving a presentation about the evolution and design of traditional wooden toys. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a historian called Sonia Edwards giving a presentation about the evolution and design of traditional wooden toys. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Sonia Edwards discusses the historical significance and craftsmanship behind traditional wooden toys.
extracts.audio_script:
Good afternoon. I’m Sonia Edwards, and I’ve spent much of my career researching the history of childhood through the objects children played with. Today, I want to focus on the enduring appeal of the wooden toy. In an age of high-tech gadgets, it’s fascinating that sales of wooden trains and blocks are actually rising. My own interest in this field wasn't sparked by a museum visit, as you might expect. It actually began when I found an [7]antique rocking horse[/7] in my aunt’s attic. It was missing its tail and much of its paint, but the carving was exquisite. It made me realize that toys are more than just playthings; they are works of art that reflect the values of the era in which they were made.

When we look at the history of toy manufacturing, wood was the primary material for centuries. However, the industry changed dramatically during the Victorian era. The most significant shift was the [8]mass production[/8] of toys in specialized factories, particularly in Germany. Before this, most toys were unique items carved by parents or local carpenters. This industrialization meant that for the first time, toys became affordable for the middle classes, not just the very wealthy. It was the birth of the toy industry as we know it today.

The design of these early toys was often surprisingly educational. For instance, many wooden puzzles from the 1800s were designed to teach children about geography. I was intrigued to learn that [9]alphabet blocks[/9] were originally intended to be used as tools for moral instruction as much as for literacy. Each side of the block would feature a letter and a corresponding virtue or biblical story. It’s a far cry from the simple 'A is for Apple' we see on modern versions.

As a researcher, I spend a lot of time in archives, but I also work closely with modern manufacturers who still use traditional methods. They face many challenges, but the most pressing concern for a contemporary wooden toy maker is the [10]safety certification[/10] process. Because wood is a natural material, ensuring that every batch of paint is non-toxic and that the wood won't splinter is a rigorous and expensive undertaking. It’s a necessary hurdle, but it makes it difficult for small-scale artisans to compete with larger corporations.

I’ve also been looking at the psychology of play. Why do children still reach for a wooden car over a flashing plastic one? Many child psychologists believe it’s because wood offers a [11]tactile experience[/11] that synthetic materials cannot match. The weight, the warmth, and even the smell of the wood provide a grounding sensory input that helps children focus. It encourages what we call 'open-ended play', where the child provides the imagination rather than the toy providing the entertainment.

One of the most rewarding parts of my job is restoring old items. Last year, I worked on a set of wooden soldiers from the 1920s. Under layers of grime, I discovered that the artist had used [12]crushed beetles[/12] to create a specific shade of vibrant red for the uniforms. It was a common technique at the time, but seeing it preserved after a hundred years was incredible. It’s these small details that tell us so much about the chemistry and trade routes of the past.

Despite the rise of digital entertainment, I am optimistic about the future of traditional toys. I believe we are seeing a [13]cultural shift[/13] towards more sustainable and long-lasting products. Parents are increasingly looking for toys that can be passed down through generations rather than something that will be in a bin by next Christmas. The durability of wood is its greatest strength.

To conclude, my team is currently working with the National Archive to digitize a collection of [14]blueprint drawings[/14] from the UK’s oldest toy factory. We hope this will allow a new generation of designers to study the proportions and mechanisms that made these toys so successful. Thank you for listening, and I’m happy to take any questions.
